title: Aquarium Glass Thickness Calculator
tagline: Calculate the minimum safe glass thickness for any aquarium based on dimensions, water pressure, and material safety factors.
intro: Enter your tank dimensions, glass type, and safety factor to find the minimum required glass thickness and the nearest standard panel size.

error.invalid: Please enter valid positive values for all fields.

about.heading: About the Aquarium Glass Thickness Calculator
about.body:
Building or buying an aquarium requires careful attention to structural integrity. The glass panels must withstand the full hydrostatic pressure of the water column without cracking, bowing, or — in a catastrophic failure — shattering explosively and flooding your home or public space. Calculating the correct minimum glass thickness before construction is a fundamental safety requirement, not an optional refinement.

Hydrostatic pressure is the pressure exerted by a fluid at rest. For a freshwater aquarium, the maximum pressure occurs at the very bottom of the water column and equals ρ × g × h, where ρ is the water density (approximately 1,000 kg/m³ for fresh water, up to 1,025 kg/m³ for salt water), g is gravitational acceleration (9.81 m/s²), and h is the depth of water in metres. This pressure is uniform across the entire bottom panel but also acts against the side panels with a distribution that is highest at the base and zero at the waterline — which is why thicker glass is always needed for taller tanks even when the footprint is the same.

The structural calculation for a rectangular glass panel under uniform hydrostatic pressure follows the theory of plates. The critical dimension is the shorter panel side (the dimension a), because shorter panels are stiffer and the bending moment for a given pressure scales with the square of the shorter span. The formula used here is derived from the plate-bending equation: t = a × √(3P / (4σ)), where t is the required thickness, a is the shorter panel dimension in metres, P is the maximum pressure in pascals, and σ is the allowable bending stress of the glass in pascals.

The allowable bending stress is determined by dividing the material's characteristic bending strength by the safety factor. For standard float (soda-lime) glass, the characteristic bending strength is approximately 45 MPa. For thermally tempered glass, it is roughly 120 MPa — which is why tempered glass allows thinner panels for the same tank size. Laminated glass has an intermediate strength of around 52 MPa; cast acrylic (perspex) has an ultimate tensile strength of roughly 65 MPa. Applying a safety factor of 3.8 is standard for structural glass in contact with water (DIN 1288 and similar standards), providing a generous margin against hidden flaws, impact loads, and long-term fatigue.

The calculated minimum thickness is then rounded up to the nearest commercially available glass panel thickness: 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12, 15, 19, or 25 mm. Always source glass from a reputable supplier and specify the exact type (annealed float, heat-strengthened, or toughened/tempered). Tempered glass must be cut to size before tempering — it cannot be cut after, and post-process damage such as scratching or drilling can cause spontaneous failure. Acrylic panels are lighter and less brittle than glass, but they scratch more easily and bow outward under pressure more noticeably than glass of similar thickness.

This calculator is suitable for planning purposes and educational use. For tanks above approximately 500 litres, public aquariums, or load-bearing structural installations, consult a qualified structural engineer and follow the applicable local building codes and glass standards.

examples.heading: Example Calculations
examples.intro: Click any example to load a typical aquarium specification and see the required glass thickness.

items.input: 60×40×40 cm, 90% water, standard glass, SF=3.8, fresh water
items.note: A 96-litre starter tank. 40 cm panel width and 36 cm water depth with standard glass at SF=3.8 gives a calculated 5.98 mm — rounded up to the standard 6 mm panel.

items.input: 100×50×50 cm, 90% water, tempered glass, SF=3.8, salt water
items.note: A 225-litre saltwater tank. Tempered glass is 2.7× stronger than float glass, reducing the required thickness; 6 mm is the practical standard minimum for this size.

items.input: 150×60×60 cm, 90% water, standard glass, SF=3.8, fresh water
items.note: A large 486-litre display tank. The 60 cm panel span and 54 cm water depth drives the requirement up to 12 mm standard glass; a centre brace is strongly recommended.

items.input: 40×25×30 cm, 90% water, standard glass, SF=3.8, fresh water
items.note: A compact nano tank. Low water column means 4 mm float glass is structurally sufficient; the front/back panels (40×30 cm) govern with span 30 cm, giving ≈3.9 mm calculated.

howto.heading: How to Use the Glass Thickness Calculator
steps: Enter the internal length, width, and height of your aquarium in centimetres. Use internal measurements — the water-contact dimensions, not the external frame.
Set the water level percentage. Tanks are rarely filled to 100%; typical values are 85–95%. The water depth determines the hydrostatic pressure.
Select the glass type. Tempered glass allows thinner panels but must be cut before tempering. Standard float glass can be cut to size, drilled, and edged after purchase.
Set the safety factor (default 3.8, as per DIN glass standards) and water density (1,000 kg/m³ for fresh water, 1,025 for sea water).
Click Calculate. The results show the calculated minimum thickness and the nearest commercially available standard thickness to order.

items.q: Why does a taller tank need thicker glass even if it is the same volume?
items.a: Hydrostatic pressure increases with depth. A taller tank has a higher water column, so the pressure at the base is greater. The bending stress in the glass panels scales with the square of the water height and the span between supports. Doubling the water height roughly quadruples the required glass thickness (in the simplified formula), which is why very tall tanks need disproportionately thick panels or internal bracing to reduce the unsupported span.

items.q: What is a safety factor and why is 3.8 used?
items.a: A safety factor is the ratio between a material's characteristic breaking strength and the maximum stress it is designed to experience in service. A safety factor of 3.8 means the glass would need to experience 3.8 times the calculated design pressure before failure. This margin accounts for hidden flaws in glass, impact loads from cleaning equipment, manufacturing variability, and long-term stress relaxation. European glass standards (DIN 1288) and many aquarium engineering references cite 3.8 as the recommended safety factor for aquarium glass.

items.q: What is the difference between tempered and standard float glass?
items.a: Standard float (annealed) glass has a characteristic bending strength of about 45 MPa. Tempered (toughened) glass is heat-treated to introduce surface compression, raising the characteristic bending strength to around 120 MPa — nearly three times stronger. When tempered glass does break, it shatters into small, relatively harmless granules rather than sharp shards, which is an important safety advantage. The key drawback is that tempered glass must be fully processed (cut, drilled, edged) before tempering, because any post-process cutting will cause it to shatter.

items.q: Can I use acrylic instead of glass?
items.a: Cast acrylic is a valid alternative, especially for curved tanks, marine installations, and public viewing windows. It is lighter than glass, does not shatter into sharp shards, and has good impact resistance. However, acrylic scratches far more easily than glass, can yellow with UV exposure over years, and bows outward noticeably under pressure compared to glass of similar thickness. The calculator includes acrylic as a material option using its characteristic tensile strength of approximately 65 MPa.

items.q: Should I add a centre brace to the top of the tank?
items.a: A centre brace (also called a cross brace or eurobrace) dramatically reduces the effective span of the glass panels by providing a midpoint support. For tanks wider than about 60 cm, a centre brace allows thinner glass by halving the unsupported width dimension used in the stress calculation. The calculator does not model centre braces, so if you plan to include one, you can reduce the effective shorter dimension by half when entering dimensions to get a more accurate thickness estimate.

items.q: Is this calculator suitable for large public aquarium installations?
items.a: This calculator provides a good first estimate based on standard plate-bending theory and is reliable for typical home and hobbyist aquariums up to a few hundred litres. For large public installations, tanks above 1,000 litres, or any safety-critical structure, the results should be verified by a qualified structural engineer using full finite-element analysis and the applicable national standards. Public aquarium viewing panels may also need to meet impact-resistance (safety glazing) requirements beyond static pressure considerations.
